KLL Sketches Count-Min Sketch TopK HyperLogLog Bloom Filters Intrinsic Columns
Blocks
Block Scoring Block Format
Performance
Benchmarks Production Results

Block Format

Overview

A blockpack block holds ~2000 spans in a hybrid row/column layout. Logically it is rows (spans), physically it is columns (one array per attribute). This gives the best of both: row-level predicate evaluation AND columnar compression.

Each block is a self-contained, immutable unit. Blocks are written once, never modified, and fetched in a single I/O operation. The columnar layout enables the query engine to decode only the columns referenced by the query — a technique called lazy column decode.

Column Layout

Each block is a sequence of encoded columns. The on-disk format is:

  1. Block header — 24 bytes: magic, version, span count, column count
  2. Column metadata array — per column: (name<em>len, name, col</em>type, data<em>offset, data</em>len)
  3. Column data — per column: enc<em>version[1] + enc</em>kind[1] + kind<em>specific</em>data

Within each column's data:

  • Presence bitmap — RLE-encoded bitset indicating which rows have a value (handles sparse/null columns)
  • Encoded values — the actual data in the chosen encoding
  • Zstd compression — applied per column; buffers are pooled per-block and reused across all columns
Block bytes
├── Block header (24 bytes: magic, version, span_count, col_count)
├── Column metadata[0]: span:duration   (name_len, name, type, offset, len)
├── Column metadata[1]: resource.service.name
├── Column metadata[2]: span.http.status_code
├── ...
├── Column data[0]: span:duration
│   ├── enc_version[1] + enc_kind[1] = DeltaUint64
│   ├── presence bitmap (RLE: all 2000 present → 3 bytes)
│   └── delta-encoded uint64 array (zstd compressed)
├── Column data[1]: resource.service.name
│   ├── enc_version[1] + enc_kind[1] = Dictionary
│   ├── presence bitmap (RLE: all 2000 present → 3 bytes)
│   └── dictionary: [auth, web, api] + index array (zstd)
├── Column data[2]: span.http.status_code (sparse — 60% null)
│   ├── enc_version[1] + enc_kind[1] = SparseDictionary
│   ├── presence bitmap (RLE: marks 800 non-null rows → ~20 bytes)
│   └── dictionary + index for 800 values only (zstd)
└── ...

Encoding Types

The writer selects an encoding per column based on the column's type, cardinality, and sparsity. All encodings are followed by Zstd compression.

KindNameBest forProsCons
1DictionaryLow-cardinality strings (service names, HTTP methods)Excellent — 10–100× for repeated valuesPoor for high-cardinality columns
2Sparse DictionarySame but >50% nullSkips null rows entirelyPresence bitmap adds slight overhead
5Delta Uint64Monotonic integers (timestamps, offsets)4–8× — small deltas compress very wellOnly works for uint64
6RLE IndexesBoolean-like enums (span.kind: CLIENT/SERVER/INTERNAL)Near-zero storage for long runsWasted on random distributions
7Sparse RLESame + sparseBoth benefits combinedRarely chosen
8XOR BytesTrace IDs, span IDs (adjacent values share structure)2–4× over rawRequires full decode for lookup
9Sparse XORSame + sparse——
10Prefix BytesURL paths, HTTP targetsExcellent for long common prefixesOnly worthwhile if prefix >4 bytes
11Sparse PrefixSame + sparse——
12Delta Dictionarytrace:id column specifically — sorted ULIDsOptimal for monotonically-increasing trace IDsSpecialized
13Sparse Delta DictRare——

After encoding, each column is individually Zstd-compressed, achieving another 3–5× reduction.

Read Patterns

Single I/O per block

Blockpack targets object storage (S3/GCS/Azure) where request latency is 50–100 ms. Every block is always read in a single HTTP request. No partial reads, no per-column fetches. The entire block payload ([]byte) is fetched, then columns are decoded in-memory.

This is a hard invariant. Adding per-column I/O would cause 10–120× more API calls and was explicitly removed in prior versions.

MetricGoodWarningCritical
io_ops< 500500–1000> 1000
bytes/io> 100 KB10–100 KB< 10 KB

Lazy column decode

After fetching the block, parseBlockColumnsReuse only decodes columns referenced by the query (wantColumns). Columns not in wantColumns register their metadata but do not decode values. Presence-only decode is available before full value decode, and sparse index expansion is deferred to first access.

A query for duration > 5ms on a block with 40 columns only decodes the span:duration column.

GET /block/blk-003 → []byte (full block, 1 I/O)
    │
    └─ parseBlockColumnsReuse(wantColumns: [span:duration, resource.service.name])
           ├── decode column 0: span:duration          ← needed by predicate
           ├── decode column 1: resource.service.name  ← needed by predicate
           ├── SKIP column 2: span.http.status_code    ← metadata registered, values not decoded
           ├── SKIP column 3: span.kind
           └── SKIP column N: ...

Coalesced reads

When multiple adjacent blocks are scheduled for reading, blockpack merges their byte ranges into a single HTTP request (reader/coalesce.go). This avoids the per-request latency overhead for blocks that happen to be stored contiguously.

Merge algorithm: sort blocks by offset, then merge adjacent pairs if all three conditions hold:

  • gap ≤ MaxGapBytes (default: 4 MB)
  • wasteRatio ≤ MaxWasteRatio (default: 100% — waste up to 2× useful bytes)
  • mergedLen ≤ MaxReadBytes (default: 8 MB per request)

The merged request fetches all block bytes in one round-trip; individual block payloads are sliced out in-memory.

Without coalescing:
  GET /file, range=[0, 128KB]     → blk-001
  GET /file, range=[128KB, 256KB] → blk-002    ← 2 × 50ms latency = 100ms
  GET /file, range=[256KB, 384KB] → blk-003

With coalescing (gap=0KB < 4MB, merged=384KB < 8MB):
  GET /file, range=[0, 384KB]     → blk-001 + blk-002 + blk-003  ← 1 × 50ms
  slice in-memory → blk-001 [0:128KB], blk-002 [128KB:256KB], blk-003 [256KB:384KB]

Index-Based Read Prioritization

The query planner scores each candidate block using TopK, HLL, and CMS data before issuing any I/O (queryplanner/scoring.go, planner.go:setToSortedByScore). Blocks are sorted by timestamp (primary) then score descending (secondary). Higher-scored blocks — those more likely to contain matching spans — are fetched first.

This enables early termination: once enough matching spans have been found (up to the result limit), remaining lower-scored blocks are never read. A query with a limit of 10 results might fetch 2 high-scoring blocks (finding 10 matches) and skip the remaining 8 blocks entirely.

Score formula:

score = sum(CMS_frequency) / max(HLL_cardinality, 1)

Higher frequency + lower cardinality = higher selectivity = fetch first.

Source

FilePurpose
internal/modules/blockio/writer/writer</em>block.goBlock encoding and column selection
internal/modules/blockio/reader/reader.goGetBlockWithBytes, parseBlockColumnsReuse
internal/modules/queryplanner/scoring.goBlock scoring formula
internal/modules/executor/stream.goBlock iteration order
